learnings.title: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH
learnings.body: Multiple state mutation paths cause corruption. Solution: ONE write function only. In streaming, only appendToMessage() may write message text. All map(... text updates) are banned inside the streaming loop.

learnings.title: STREAM ID IS MANDATORY
learnings.body: Without a streamId, stale streams corrupt UI state. Each delivery generates a unique streamId = newMessageId(). currentStreamId is set to this value. Every chunk checks: if (streamId !== currentStreamId) return. No old stream may write to new state.

learnings.title: BREAK IS NOT SAFE — USE RETURN
learnings.body: break exits only the for loop and continues method execution. return exits the function entirely, jumping to finally. For stale stream detection and abort guards inside a try/finally structure, use return. break allows finalization code to run — which is wrong for stale streams.

learnings.title: ABORT SIGNAL MUST BE CHECKED INSIDE THE LOOP
learnings.body: controller.signal.aborted must be checked as the FIRST thing in each loop iteration, before any state write. If aborted: break (generator will throw AbortError from the fetch side), or return if abort was detected between iterations. Post-loop: if (controller.signal.aborted) return — do not finalize.

learnings.title: UI MUST NOT INFER STATE FROM TEXT
learnings.body: Checking !msg.text?.trim() to detect pre-first-token state is unreliable — whitespace tokens would break it. Use an explicit flag: hasReceivedFirstToken: boolean on the ChatMessage. appendToMessage sets this to true on first write. UI checks !msg.hasReceivedFirstToken for Typing... indicator.

learnings.title: BACKEND FAILURE IS NOT MODEL FAILURE
learnings.body: The model generated correct output. The control flow (state management, streaming loop, stream ID enforcement) broke the system. Always separate model errors from delivery errors in diagnosis.

learnings.title: STREAMING IS A CONCURRENCY PROBLEM
learnings.body: Streaming is not a UI problem and not a generation problem. It is a concurrency control problem. Stream ownership (streamId), write isolation (appendToMessage), abort propagation (AbortController), and finalization guards must all be correct simultaneously.

learnings.title: TESTING MUST BE REAL RUNTIME
learnings.body: Browser-only or mock tests give false confidence. Real validation requires: Tauri app running, backend on port 8000, real model (ollama), Playwright connecting to the actual window. Use tests/tauri-playwright/streaming-correctness.spec.ts.

learnings.title: STATE FINALIZATION IS CRITICAL
learnings.body: An old stream must NEVER finalize new state. Finalization (streaming = false, currentStreamId = null) must be gated: success path: if (streamId === currentStreamId). finally path: if (streamId !== undefined && streamId === currentStreamId). This prevents a completed/aborted stream from resetting an active one.

learnings.title: MOST FAILURES ARE CONTROL FLOW, NOT AI
learnings.body: The language model worked correctly throughout. Every failure was in: state mutation order, missing ownership checks, break vs return semantics, accumulated variable as parallel state. Correct streaming = correct concurrency discipline.

architecture_decisions.decision: appendToMessage(messageId, text) as the single write path
architecture_decisions.rationale: Targeting by ID eliminates last-message ambiguity. Single function means one place to guard, one place to set hasReceivedFirstToken.
finally block needs streamId to enforce the finalization guard. Variables declared inside try are not accessible in finally.
Decoupled from message ID so stream ownership is independent of message identity. One stream per delivery, not one per message.
